subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'The government has decided to increase the tax on luxury cars to reduce fiscal deficit.'
Assumption I: Luxury car buyers can afford to pay more tax.
Assumption II: Increasing tax on luxury cars will significantly contribute to reducing the deficit.

options.text: Both I and II are implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are implicit

step_by_step: The decision to tax luxury cars implies they have the capacity to pay (I).
The motive is to reduce the deficit, implying the government believes this action will help (II).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Should students be allowed to use mobile phones in schools?'
Argument I: Yes, it allows them to access educational resources and stay in touch with parents.
Argument II: No, it leads to distractions and cyberbullying.

options.text: Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are strong

step_by_step: I provides a logical benefit (safety/resources).
II highlights a logical risk (distraction/bullying). Both are valid concerns.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'All the students in the class passed the examination.'
Conclusion I: Some students failed the examination.
Conclusion II: The teacher taught the class well.

step_by_step: I contradicts the statement (All passed).
II might be true but is not a direct logical conclusion of the fact that they passed (they could have studied on their own).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'The company has decided to offer a 20% discount on all its products to boost sales.'
Assumption I: People will be attracted by the discount.
Assumption II: The company's sales have been low recently.

options.text: Both I and II are implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit

step_by_step: The company assumes people like discounts (I).
While sales might be low, the statement only says the goal is to 'boost' them, which could also apply to healthy sales wanting to grow further (II is not necessarily implicit).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Should computers be used to teach children in primary schools?'
Argument I: Yes, it makes learning fun and interactive.
Argument II: No, it reduces the social interaction between children and teachers.

options.text: Both I and II are strong
Neither I nor II is strong

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are strong

step_by_step: I provides a pedagogical benefit.
II provides a developmental concern. Both are valid 'strong' points for a debate.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Reading books regularly improves vocabulary.'
Conclusion I: Those who do not read books have poor vocabulary.
Conclusion II: Reading is the only way to improve vocabulary.

step_by_step: I assumes a negative which the statement doesn't support.
II uses the word 'only' which is an extreme and usually incorrect logical jump.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Course of Action: 'The number of road accidents has increased due to poor road conditions.'
Action I: The government should repair the roads immediately.
Action II: People should stop using vehicles.

step_by_step: Repairing the root cause (roads) is a logical action.
Stopping vehicle use is extreme and impractical (II).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'If you are a good swimmer, you will enjoy scuba diving.'
Conclusion I: All those who enjoy scuba diving are good swimmers.
Conclusion II: If you don't enjoy scuba diving, you are not a good swimmer.

step_by_step: If P then Q does not mean If Q then P (I is wrong).

If P then Q means If not Q then not P. However, in common speech/logic exams, this specific phrasing of 'If you don't enjoy... you are not good' is often treated as invalid unless stated as a formal logical contrapositive. Here, I and II are common logical fallacies.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Every city in the country should have a waste management plant.'
Assumption I: Waste management is a major problem in every city.
Assumption II: Waste management plants help in reducing pollution.

options.text: Both I and II are implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are implicit

step_by_step: A recommendation implies a problem exists (I) and the solution works (II).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Should the death penalty be abolished?'
Argument I: Yes, it is inhumane and does not deter crime.
Argument II: No, it is a just punishment for the most heinous crimes.

options.text: Neither I nor II is strong
Both I and II are strong

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are strong

step_by_step: I and II represent the two major logical pillars of the debate.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'The price of petroleum has increased in the international market.'
Conclusion I: The government will increase the price of fuel locally.
Conclusion II: People will stop using vehicles.

step_by_step: While the government 'might' increase prices, it's not a logical 'must' (I).
People won't stop using vehicles entirely (II).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Smoking is injurious to health.'
Assumption I: Non-smokers are healthy.
Assumption II: Health-conscious people do not smoke.

options.text: Both I and II are implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit

step_by_step: The warning is for the sake of health, implying those who care for health will heed it (II).
Non-smokers might have other health issues (I is false).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'If you want a quality product, buy from Brand X.'
Conclusion I: Brand X is the only brand that sells quality products.
Conclusion II: Brand X is a reliable brand.

step_by_step: The recommendation implies reliability for quality (II).
'Only' makes I logically incorrect.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Exercise daily to keep fit.'
Assumption I: Fit people exercise daily.
Assumption II: Exercise is necessary for fitness.

options.text: Both I and II are implicit
Neither I nor II is implicit

step_by_step: The advice implies a causal link (II).
Some fit people might have high metabolism or other means (I is not necessarily implicit).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question_text:
Statement: 'Should there be a limit on the number of children a couple can have?'
Argument I: Yes, to control the population explosion.
Argument II: No, it interferes with personal freedom.

options.text: Both I and II are strong
Neither I nor II is strong

solution.final_answer: Both I and II are strong

step_by_step: Both represent the core conflict in population policy logic.
